proposals.title: Systematic Functional Dissection of Rare Variants in PDAC-Associated Genes to Distinguish Benign Polymorphisms from Pathogenic Mutations
proposals.motivation: In cancer genomics, particularly in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), the clinical interpretation of rare germline and somatic variants is critical for patient risk assessment and therapeutic targeting. Many variants of uncertain significance (VUS) are classified based on population frequency and computational predictions, which can erroneously label benign polymorphisms linked to a true causal variant as pathogenic. This leads to misinformed clinical decisions and obscures the true genetic drivers of disease.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant fraction of rare variants identified in known PDAC-associated genes (e.g., KRAS, TP53, CDKN2A, SMAD4) in patient cohorts are benign passenger polymorphisms in linkage disequilibrium with undiscovered functional variants, rather than being directly pathogenic.
proposals.method: We will perform deep, targeted sequencing of PDAC-associated loci in a large patient cohort to identify rare variants. For each variant, we will conduct high-throughput functional assays in relevant cell lines (e.g., pancreatic ductal cells) using CRISPR/Cas9-mediated homology-directed repair to create isogenic cell lines harboring each specific variant. Functional impact will be assessed using established assays for proliferation, colony formation, and pathway activation (e.g., MAPK signaling). Statistical and bioinformatic analysis, including haplotype reconstruction and linkage disequilibrium mapping, will correlate functional readouts with variant status and co-segregating genetic markers.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a validated functional map of rare variants in PDAC genes, directly informing clinical variant interpretation. It will refine genetic risk models by distinguishing true driver mutations from linked polymorphisms, leading to more accurate patient stratification. The resource of isogenic cell lines will serve as a platform for future mechanistic studies.
proposals.novelty: While linkage is a known confounder in genetics, this study directly and systematically tests the functional impact of individual rare variants in their native genomic context in PDAC-relevant cells, moving beyond computational prediction to empirical validation. It integrates precise genome editing with detailed haplotype analysis specifically for pancreatic cancer variants.

proposals.title: Comparative Genomic Hybridization Screening for NKX2-8 and Syntenic Region Copy Number Variations in Human Neural Tube Defect Patients
proposals.motivation: Neural tube defects (NTDs) are severe congenital malformations with a complex, incompletely understood genetic etiology. Prior work in mouse models, including linkage maps of chromosome 18, has identified Nkx2-8 as a candidate gene for curly tail phenotypes, a model of spinal NTDs. Translating these findings to human patients is a critical next step for understanding disease pathogenesis and identifying potential genetic risk factors.
proposals.hypothesis: Human patients with NTDs harbor recurrent copy number variations (CNVs) in the genomic region containing NKX2-8 and in syntenic regions on human chromosomes 5 and 18, which are evolutionarily conserved from the mouse model loci.
proposals.method: We will apply array-based comparative genomic hybridization (aCGH) to genomic DNA from a well-characterized cohort of human NTD patients and matched controls. The custom-designed array will be densely tiled across the NKX2-8 locus on human chromosome 14 and the syntenic regions on chromosomes 5 and 18, based on pre-2026 linkage conservation data. CNV calling will be performed using established algorithms (e.g., CBS segmentation, ADM-2). Candidate CNVs will be validated using quantitative PCR, a technique previously used in the lab for in vivo cloning and quantification.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly test the relevance of a mouse NTD candidate gene in human disease, providing concrete evidence for or against NKX2-8 pathway involvement. It will generate a novel dataset of CNVs in NTD patients within these specific genomic intervals, potentially identifying new susceptibility loci or rare pathogenic variants.
proposals.novelty: While mouse genetics strongly implicate Nkx2-8, systematic screening for structural variants in this specific gene and its conserved syntenic regions has not been comprehensively performed in human NTD cohorts, representing a direct translational application of comparative genomic findings.

proposals.title: Chromosomal Mapping of Amplified Oncogenes in Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma Using Rodent-Human Hybrid Cell Panels
proposals.motivation: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is characterized by frequent copy number alterations and gene amplifications, such as KRAS, MYC, and GATA6, which drive tumorigenesis and therapy resistance. Pre-2026, the specific chromosomal loci harboring these amplified units are often inferred from sequencing but not definitively mapped to physical chromosomes in a functional cellular context. Somatic cell genetics provides a direct method for such assignment, complementing genomic data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using panels of rodent-human hybrid cells retaining specific human chromosomes will allow the unambiguous assignment of amplified PDAC oncogenes and resistance markers to their resident human chromosomes, validating and extending findings from genomic studies.
proposals.method: We will generate a library of somatic cell hybrids by fusing PDAC cell lines (e.g., PANC-1, MIA PaCa-2) with Chinese hamster ovary (CHO) or mouse A9 cells, followed by selection and karyotyping to define retained human chromosomes. DNA from hybrid clones will be screened via PCR and Southern blotting for known amplified genes (e.g., using probes for KRAS, MYC). Co-segregation analysis of gene presence/absence with specific human chromosomes across the hybrid panel will map the genes. Fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) on positive hybrids will provide confirmatory sub-chromosomal localization.
proposals.contribution: This work will create a functional map linking critical PDAC amplicons to their host chromosomes, providing orthogonal validation to NGS-based copy number calls. It will establish a renewable resource of characterized hybrid cells for future functional studies of gene dosage. The findings may reveal common chromosomal 'hotspots' for amplification in PDAC, informing models of genomic instability.
proposals.novelty: While rodent-human hybrids are classic tools for gene mapping, their application is novel for systematically assigning amplified oncogenes from established PDAC cell lines, moving beyond correlative genomics to direct physical chromosome assignment in a cellular context. This approach is distinct from purely computational analyses of sequencing data.

proposals.title: A Functional Genomics and Radiomics Approach to Define the Combinatorial Impact of Tumor Suppressor Gene Deletions in Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma
proposals.motivation: The Vogelstein lab's 'Cancer Gene' concept established that tumorigenesis requires both oncogene activation and tumor suppressor gene (TSG) inactivation. While the functional impact of oncogenic mutations is well-studied, the combinatorial effects of TSG deletions, which are ubiquitous in cancers like PDAC, remain poorly understood. A systematic analysis of how specific combinations of TSG losses drive tumor progression and influence tumor morphology is critically needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, recurrent combinations of tumor suppressor gene deletions in PDAC drive distinct tumor phenotypes, which can be quantified through integrated genomic and radiomic analysis.
proposals.method: We will apply CRISPR-Cas9 to generate a panel of human PDAC cell lines with combinatorial deletions of key TSGs (e.g., TP53, SMAD4, CDKN2A). These engineered lines will be orthotopically implanted into mice. Tumors will be analyzed using high-resolution micro-CT, and radiomic features will be extracted using the PyRadiomics platform. We will correlate specific genetic deletion combinations with distinct radiomic signatures and validate findings in human PDAC patient cohorts using existing genomic (TCGA) and matched CT imaging data.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a functional map linking specific TSG deletion combinations to quantifiable tumor phenotypes. It will provide a novel framework for interpreting clinical imaging through a genetic lens, potentially identifying non-invasive radiomic biomarkers for specific genetic subtypes of PDAC to guide therapy.
proposals.novelty: This study inverts the typical focus from oncogene activation to TSG loss and uniquely integrates precise genetic engineering in vivo with quantitative radiomics, a combination not yet applied to dissect the functional impact of combinatorial TSG deletions.

proposals.title: Genomic Amplicon Size as a Prognostic Biomarker in Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma: Correlating Physical Amplification Extent with Clinical and Pathological Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Chromosomal amplifications are frequent drivers in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), but current analyses focus primarily on the identity of amplified oncogenes (e.g., KRAS, MYC). The physical size of the amplified genomic region, which can vary from focal events to whole-arm gains, is rarely quantified or correlated with disease course. Given that larger amplicons may encompass more co-amplified genes and regulatory elements, they could drive more aggressive tumor biology, offering a novel, quantifiable genomic metric for risk stratification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the physical size (in megabases) of somatically amplified chromosomal regions in PDAC is positively correlated with established markers of tumor aggressiveness, including pathological grade, stage at diagnosis, and overall survival.
proposals.method: We will analyze whole-genome sequencing data from PDAC patient cohorts (e.g., TCGA, ICGC). Using pre-2026 algorithms like GISTIC2.0 to identify recurrent amplifications, we will then employ custom scripts to precisely map the genomic boundaries of each amplification event in individual tumors. The size (Mb) of each event will be calculated. Statistical correlation will be performed between amplicon size and clinical variables (stage, grade, survival) using Cox proportional hazards and logistic regression models, controlling for known confounders like tumor purity and ploidy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish amplicon size as a novel, quantitative genomic feature with potential prognostic value in PDAC. It will move beyond gene-centric views to a structural genomic perspective, potentially identifying a subset of patients with highly unstable, aggressive tumors. The findings could inform future studies on the mechanistic link between amplicon architecture and therapeutic resistance.
proposals.novelty: While the role of specific amplified genes is well-studied, the direct correlation between the physical scale of the amplification and clinical severity is an unexplored dimension in PDAC genomics. This proposal leverages existing WGS data to test a simple but previously unaddressed structural metric.

proposals.title: Genome-wide mapping of nuclear matrix attachment regions (MARs) in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma reveals novel structural drivers of oncogene dysregulation
proposals.motivation: Nuclear matrix attachment regions (MARs) are critical for chromatin organization and gene regulation, but a comprehensive, high-resolution map in human cancers is lacking. In pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), known for its complex genomic alterations and therapeutic resistance, understanding the 3D genome architecture could reveal novel mechanisms of oncogene activation and tumor suppressor silencing that are not explained by sequence mutations alone.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that PDAC cells harbor a distinct, disease-specific landscape of MARs, and that alterations in these anchorage points are a major determinant of the dysregulated transcriptional programs driving tumor progression.
proposals.method: We will perform a genome-wide MAR analysis using a modified high-throughput sequencing approach (MAR-Seq), adapting established protocols for isolating nuclear matrix-bound DNA. Using patient-derived PDAC cell lines and matched normal pancreatic ductal cells, we will identify MARs via their resistance to high-salt and detergent extraction. Data will be analyzed using established bioinformatics pipelines (e.g., MACS2 for peak calling) and integrated with pre-existing ChIP-seq (H3K27ac, CTCF) and RNA-seq datasets from the same models to correlate MAR positions with regulatory activity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive MAR map in PDAC, providing a foundational resource for the cancer genomics community. It will identify novel structural elements potentially involved in the dysregulation of key PDAC driver genes like KRAS, TP53, CDKN2A, and SMAD4, offering new targets for epigenetic diagnostics or therapies.
proposals.novelty: While MARs have been studied in model systems, this proposal applies a systematic, genome-wide mapping strategy directly to a lethal human cancer, integrating it with orthogonal functional genomic data to move beyond cataloging towards mechanistic insight in disease.

proposals.title: Defining Immunization Thresholds: A Systematic Prime-Boost Matrix to Convert Non-Immunogenic Model Antigens into Protective Cancer Vaccines
proposals.motivation: A major obstacle in cancer vaccine development is the poor immunogenicity of many tumor-associated antigens (TAAs), which are often self-antigens that fail to break immune tolerance. While using foreign antigens can bypass central tolerance, empirical vaccine schedules often fail, suggesting that antigen foreignness alone is insufficient without precise immunization parameters. This study addresses the critical gap in understanding how dose, timing, and antigen load interact to define a threshold for initiating a de novo cytotoxic T lymphocyte (CTL) response against an otherwise ignored antigen.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that there exists a quantifiable, multi-dimensional threshold—defined by antigen expression level, initial immunizing cell number, and booster schedule—above which irradiated, foreign antigen-expressing tumor cells can prime protective anti-tumor immunity, even when the antigen is non-immunogenic under standard vaccination protocols.
proposals.method: We will use a syngeneic mouse model of colorectal cancer (derived from Apc/Min+ backgrounds, aligning with Vogelstein's expertise) engineered to express a model foreign antigen (e.g., ovalbumin). A factorial design will test a matrix of variables: antigen expression (low/medium/high via titration of expression vectors), prime dose (10^3 to 10^6 irradiated cells), and boost schedule (intervals from 7 to 21 days, 1-3 boosts). Immune readouts will include antigen-specific tetramer+ CD8+ T cell quantification by flow cytometry, IFN-γ ELISpot, and in vivo tumor challenge. Data will be analyzed using multivariate regression to identify significant interaction terms and threshold values.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a generalizable, data-driven framework for optimizing cancer vaccine regimens based on quantifiable antigen properties. It will move the field from binary classifications of antigen immunogenicity to a predictive model of immunization success. The identified thresholds could directly inform the design of clinical trials using genetically modified cellular vaccines.
proposals.novelty: While necessity of foreign antigens is established, this systematic approach to define the *sufficient conditions* for their efficacy is novel. It applies a rigorous, multi-parameter optimization—common in genetics and pharmacology—to the problem of vaccine schedule design, moving beyond empirical testing.

proposals.title: Comparative Immunogenicity of Whole-Cell versus Purified Antigen Vaccines in a Genetically Engineered Mouse Model of Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma
proposals.motivation: Cancer vaccines using whole, transfected tumor cells have shown promise in preclinical models but face significant translational hurdles related to manufacturing, safety, and standardization. The field critically lacks a direct, head-to-head comparison of whole-cell immunization against defined, purified tumor antigens in a genetically relevant, autochthonous cancer model. Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is a lethal malignancy with few neoantigens, making the choice of immunogen critical for vaccine design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that immunization with purified, recombinant HA-tagged mutant KRAS (G12D) protein, or immunodominant HA-KRAS peptides, will elicit a tumor-specific cytotoxic T lymphocyte (CTL) response and protective efficacy comparable to, or superior to, vaccination with whole cells transfected to express the same HA-KRAS fusion antigen.
proposals.method: We will utilize the LSL-KrasG12D/+; LSL-Trp53R172H/+; Pdx-1-Cre (KPC) autochthonous PDAC mouse model. Cohorts will be immunized with: 1) irradiated, HA-KRAS-transfected syngeneic tumor cells; 2) purified recombinant HA-KRASG12D protein with adjuvant; 3) predicted immunodominant HA and mutant KRAS peptides. Immune responses will be quantified by ELISpot for IFN-γ, intracellular cytokine staining, and in vivo cytotoxicity assays. Tumor prevention and progression will be monitored by longitudinal ultrasound imaging. Tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes will be analyzed by flow cytometry and immunohistochemistry.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively test a core assumption in cancer vaccinology—the necessity of whole cells for immunization. It will provide a rigorous, comparative dataset on the efficacy of defined antigen vaccines in a therapeutically challenging, immunologically 'cold' tumor model. The findings will directly inform the clinical development of simpler, safer, and more reproducible vaccine formulations for cancers like PDAC.
proposals.novelty: While whole-cell vaccines have been empirically tested, a direct comparative immunogenicity study in a genetically engineered, autochthonous PDAC model—using an identical, driver oncogene antigen across platforms—has not been performed. This approach leverages precise genetic models and molecular cloning (in vivo cloning of PCR products) to isolate the variable of antigen presentation format.

proposals.title: Defining the Immunogenic Threshold for Synergistic Efficacy of Gene-Edited Neoantigen Vaccination in Pancreatic Cancer Models
proposals.motivation: Immunotherapy has limited efficacy in immunologically 'cold' tumors like pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC). While transfection strategies to introduce neoantigens are promising, their reported synergy with immune checkpoint blockade (ICB) is often confounded by variable baseline tumor immunogenicity. A systematic, quantitative framework is needed to distinguish true synergy from additive effects and to identify the permissive immunogenic context required for therapeutic success.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the synergistic effect of in vivo neoantigen transfection and ICB is not universal but requires a specific, quantifiable baseline level of intrinsic tumor immunogenicity, which can be defined using isogenic tumor models spanning an immunogenic gradient.
proposals.method: We will engineer a panel of isogenic murine PDAC cell lines (derived from KPC models) with defined mutations (e.g., Kras, p53, known neoantigens) to create a quantifiable gradient of baseline immunogenicity, measured by flow cytometry of tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) and IFN-γ ELISpot. These lines will be challenged with an adenoviral vector for in vivo transfection of a model neoantigen (e.g., SIINFEKL) combined with anti-PD-1. Multi-parameter immune profiling (mass cytometry, RNA-seq) and tumor growth kinetics will be analyzed using the Bliss independence model and a multivariate regression framework to quantify interaction effects. This will define the immunogenic threshold for synergy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, quantitative benchmark for claiming synergistic immunotherapy combinations, moving beyond observational reports. It will establish a pre-clinical framework for patient stratification by identifying the minimal immunogenic context required for a transfection therapy to succeed, directly informing future clinical trial design in PDAC.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior studies testing combinations in single, often poorly characterized models, this study uses a controlled, isogenic gradient system and formal synergy modeling to causally link baseline immunogenicity to combinatorial efficacy, preventing false claims of synergy.

proposals.title: Synergistic Enhancement of Immunogenicity in a Moderately Immunogenic Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma Model via Ex Vivo Neoantigen Transfection
proposals.motivation: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is notoriously poorly immunogenic, contributing to its resistance to immunotherapy. Pre-2026 work, including from our lab, has shown that ex vivo transfection of poorly immunogenic tumors with neoantigen-encoding mRNA can induce T-cell responses. However, it is unknown whether this strategy can synergize with the intrinsic, albeit weak, immunogenicity of certain PDAC subsets to generate a more robust and clinically relevant anti-tumor immune response.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ex vivo transfection of a moderately immunogenic PDAC model with defined neoantigens will produce a synergistic enhancement of tumor immunogenicity, leading to superior T-cell priming and tumor control compared to either the intrinsic immunogenicity or the transfection strategy alone.
proposals.method: We will utilize a genetically engineered mouse model of PDAC (e.g., KPC) and identify a moderately immunogenic subline through in vivo immunodepletion experiments and analysis of tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes. For this subline, we will perform ex vivo transfection of tumor cells using a lipid nanoparticle (LNP) formulation to deliver mRNA encoding a panel of high-affinity neoantigens previously validated in colorectal models (e.g., mutant KrasG12D). Transfected cells will be irradiated and used as a vaccine. We will assess synergy by comparing immune responses and tumor growth in mice receiving: 1) wild-type tumor cell vaccine, 2) neoantigen-transfected vaccine from a poorly immunogenic line, and 3) neoantigen-transfected vaccine from the moderately immunogenic line. Immune profiling will use flow cytometry for T-cell activation and single-cell RNA sequencing of tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel combinatorial immunotherapy strategy tailored for PDAC subsets with residual immunogenicity. It will provide mechanistic insight into how baseline tumor-immune interactions can be leveraged to enhance vaccine efficacy. The findings could directly inform the design of next-generation autologous cancer vaccines for patients with moderate T-cell infiltrates.
proposals.novelty: While neoantigen vaccination has been explored, the strategic application of transfection specifically to a tumor with pre-existing, moderate immunogenicity to test for synergy—rather than attempting to overcome complete ignorance—is a novel and rational approach grounded in cancer immunology principles.

proposals.title: Defining the Minimal Immunogenic Unit: A Comparative Analysis of Whole-Cell, Lysate, and Exosome Vaccines Derived from HA-Tagged Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma Cells
proposals.motivation: Immunization with live or irradiated tumor cells can induce protective anti-tumor immunity, but this approach is clinically impractical for patient-specific vaccines. The identification of a simpler, acellular immunogenic component would be transformative for cancer vaccine development. This is particularly critical for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), a lethal malignancy with limited therapeutic options and known for its immunosuppressive microenvironment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that exosomes, as carriers of tumor-associated antigens and immunostimulatory molecules, will be sufficient to confer protective immunity comparable to whole-cell vaccines, whereas crude lysates will be insufficient due to a lack of organized antigen presentation.
proposals.method: We will use the KPC (KrasG12D; Trp53R172H; Pdx1-Cre) murine PDAC cell line, transfected to stably express the model antigen Hemagglutinin (HA). We will generate three vaccine preparations: irradiated whole cells, freeze-thaw lysates, and exosomes purified via differential ultracentrifugation and characterized by nanoparticle tracking analysis and immunoblotting for exosomal markers (CD63, TSG101). Syngeneic C57BL/6 mice will be vaccinated subcutaneously, followed by challenge with live, untagged KPC cells. Tumor growth will be monitored, and immune correlates (HA-specific T-cells by IFN-γ ELISpot, tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte profiling by flow cytometry) will be assessed.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test the necessity of intact cellular architecture for cancer vaccine efficacy, providing a definitive comparison of established and novel vaccine formats. It will establish whether exosomes, as a defined acellular product, are a viable platform for next-generation, neoantigen-based PDAC vaccines, potentially bridging a critical gap between preclinical models and clinical application.
proposals.novelty: While exosomes are known for immune modulation, their direct, head-to-head evaluation as a prophylactic vaccine against a genetically engineered, autochthonous PDAC model, using a defined antigenic tag for precise immune tracking, is novel. This approach moves beyond descriptive profiling to a functional test of a key translational premise.

proposals.title: High-Throughput In Vivo Screening of Xenogeneic Antigens for Protective Anti-Tumor Immunity in Pancreatic Cancer Models
proposals.motivation: Cancer immunotherapy, including vaccines, has shown limited success against immunologically 'cold' tumors like pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC). Identifying potent, immunogenic antigens is a major bottleneck. While tumor neoantigens are promising, their patient-specific nature complicates broad application. Foreign (xenogeneic) antigens can induce strong immune responses, but a systematic, unbiased method to rank their immunogenic and protective efficacy is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a high-throughput, pooled in vivo screen will identify specific foreign genes that, when expressed in tumor cells, elicit potent and protective T-cell-mediated immunity, effectively ranking xenogeneic antigens by their vaccine potential.
proposals.method: We will generate a lentiviral library of 100-200 distinct foreign genes (viral, bacterial, plant) each with a unique DNA barcode. This library will be used to stably transduce a murine PDAC cell line (e.g., derived from KPC models), creating a pooled population. This pool will be implanted into immunocompetent mice. After tumor establishment, we will sequence the barcodes from harvested tumors to quantify depletion of cells expressing highly immunogenic antigens. Parallel cohorts will be pre-immunized with the pool before challenge with parental tumor cells to assay for protective immunity. Barcode abundance will be analyzed using established algorithms for CRISPR or shRNA screen analysis (e.g., MAGeCK).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first high-throughput platform for direct, in vivo ranking of xenogeneic antigen immunogenicity. It will yield a prioritized list of candidate antigens for developing broad-spectrum cancer vaccines. The method itself will be a new tool for the immunotherapy field, analogous to genetic screens in oncology.
proposals.novelty: This adapts the high-throughput antigen discovery paradigm from infectious disease vaccinology to cancer, moving beyond candidate-driven studies. The use of a barcoded, pooled tumor cell library for in vivo immunogenicity screening is a novel application of genetic screening principles to immunotherapy.

proposals.title: A Functional Map of Foreign Gene Immunogenicity Linking Antigen Origin to Dominant Effector Mechanisms in Pancreatic Cancer Rejection
proposals.motivation: Cancer vaccines using foreign antigens to break tolerance show promise, but their efficacy is unpredictable and often fails in immunologically cold tumors like pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC). Pre-2026, while many viral and bacterial antigens are known to be immunogenic, there is no systematic understanding of which specific immune effector pathways (e.g., cytotoxic T cells vs. helper T cells) different classes of foreign genes dominantly recruit for tumor rejection. This knowledge gap hinders the rational design of vaccines and combination immunotherapies tailored to overcome the suppressive PDAC microenvironment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that foreign genes from distinct phylogenetic origins (e.g., viral vs. bacterial) and with different subcellular localizations will systematically engage non-redundant, dominant immune effector mechanisms to mediate tumor rejection in autochthonous PDAC models.
proposals.method: We will use the KPC (KrasG12D; Trp53R172H; Pdx1-Cre) autochthonous PDAC mouse model. A panel of candidate foreign genes (e.g., E. coli LacZ, influenza NP, ovalbumin, CMV pp65) will be cloned into a Sleeping Beauty transposon system for tumor cell-specific expression. Tumor rejection potency will be quantified by longitudinal imaging and survival. For each effective antigen, we will perform in vivo mechanistic studies using antibody-mediated depletion (anti-CD8, anti-CD4, anti-NK1.1) and cytokine blockade (anti-IFN-γ, anti-IL-12) in treated mice, alongside adoptive transfer experiments from immunized mice into lymphocyte-deficient Rag1-/- hosts to definitively identify the dominant effector cell population.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first functional atlas linking foreign antigen characteristics to the specific immune pathways they activate for tumor clearance. It will directly inform the selection of optimal antigen payloads for PDAC vaccines based on the desired immune response profile. Furthermore, it will provide a mechanistic rationale for pairing specific vaccine antigens with complementary checkpoint inhibitors or cytokine therapies to enhance efficacy.
proposals.novelty: While others have tested individual foreign antigens, no study has systematically compared a diverse panel in parallel within a stringent, autochthonous PDAC model while employing definitive, in vivo functional assays to map each antigen to its dominant rejection mechanism, moving beyond correlative immune profiling.

proposals.title: Lineage-Traced Mapping of Immune-Stromal Dynamics During Hyaluronan-Depletion Tumor Rejection
proposals.motivation: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is characterized by a dense, immunosuppressive stroma rich in hyaluronan (HA), which contributes to treatment resistance. While enzymatic depletion of HA can improve drug delivery and promote tumor regression in models, the precise, causal interactions between the evolving stromal cell lineages and the anti-tumor immune response remain poorly defined. A systems-level understanding of these dynamics is critical for rationally combining stromal-targeting agents with immunotherapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that HA depletion triggers a specific sequence of immune cell recruitment and activation that directly instructs the differentiation of cancer-associated fibroblast (CAF) precursors into permissive stromal subsets, and that this reciprocal crosstalk is essential for sustained tumor rejection.
proposals.method: We will employ dual-recombinase genetic mouse models (e.g., *Pdx1-Flp; FSF-Cre* or *Col1a1-FlpER*) to lineage-trace CAF precursors (e.g., pancreatic stellate cells or resident fibroblasts) upon HA depletion in established, autochthonous *Kras*; *Trp53*-driven PDAC. Concurrently, we will use *Cx3cr1-CreER*, *Lyz2-Cre*, and *Cd4-Cre* drivers to spatiotemporally profile macrophage and T cell subsets via intravital imaging and flow cytometry. Tumor and stromal cells will be isolated for single-cell RNA sequencing to define transcriptional states, and key interactions will be validated using conditional knockout of effector molecules (e.g., IFNγ) in specific immune lineages.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a causal, high-resolution map of immune-stromal interactions following a therapeutically relevant perturbation (HA depletion). It will identify which immune effector functions are necessary to reprogram the stroma and which CAF lineage commitments are permissive for an effective anti-tumor response. The findings will provide a mechanistic framework for timing and selecting immune-stromal combination therapies in PDAC.
proposals.novelty: While HA's role as a physical barrier is known, this study uniquely combines precise CAF lineage tracing with immune effector mapping to establish causality in the dynamic crosstalk that underlies tumor regression, moving beyond correlative observations.

proposals.title: Defining the Cellular and Molecular Immune Effectors Responsible for Hyaluronan-Enriched Tumor Microenvironment Rejection in Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma
proposals.motivation: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is characterized by a dense, immunosuppressive stroma rich in hyaluronan (HA), which contributes to treatment resistance and poor outcomes. While experimental HA depletion improves drug delivery and shows immunomodulatory potential, the specific immune effector mechanisms that could be harnessed to reject HA-high tumors remain undefined. Elucidating these mechanisms is critical for developing targeted immunotherapies for this lethal cancer.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that HA overexpression in PDAC cells alters the tumor immune microenvironment, leading to the recruitment and activation of a specific subset of cytotoxic T lymphocytes and/or natural killer cells, which are the primary mediators of tumor rejection in this context.
proposals.method: We will utilize a syngeneic mouse model of PDAC (derived from KPC cells) engineered to overexpress HA via transfection. Tumors will be analyzed using high-parameter flow cytometry (e.g., 18-color panels) and single-cell RNA sequencing (10x Genomics platform) to comprehensively profile tumor-infiltrating immune cells. Key effector populations will be functionally validated through in vivo depletion studies (using anti-CD8, anti-NK1.1, anti-IFN-γ antibodies) and adoptive transfer experiments. Mediator analysis will employ Luminex assays on tumor homogenates.
proposals.contribution: This work will identify the precise immune cell subsets and soluble mediators essential for rejecting HA-high PDAC, moving beyond stromal modulation to define actionable immune targets. It will provide a mechanistic foundation for combining HA-targeting strategies with specific immunotherapies, such as checkpoint inhibitors or cellular therapies, tailored to the PDAC microenvironment.
proposals.novelty: While HA's role in biomechanical resistance is established, its specific immunogenic properties and the exact effector mechanisms it engages in PDAC are unknown. This study directly links a defined stromal alteration to a concrete anti-tumor immune response, a connection not previously mapped in detail.

proposals.title: Functional Validation of Endogenous Neoantigen-Specific CD8+ T Cell Priming Following Foreign Antigen Vaccination in a Genetically Defined Mouse PDAC Model
proposals.motivation: Antigen spread is a desirable but poorly understood phenomenon in cancer immunotherapy, often correlatively observed in clinical trials. A critical, unresolved question is whether vaccination against a single foreign tumor antigen can functionally prime a de novo CD8+ T cell response against the patient's unique set of endogenous, unmutated neoantigens, which are central targets in cancers like PDAC and colorectal cancer. Mechanistic proof of such functional priming would transform our understanding of vaccine efficacy and broaden therapeutic potential.
proposals.hypothesis: Vaccination against a single, defined foreign antigen expressed by a tumor can induce a functional, tumoricidal CD8+ T cell response against endogenously processed native tumor neoantigens through epitope spreading, establishing a polyclonal anti-tumor immune response.
proposals.method: We will employ a syngeneic mouse model of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) engineered via CRISPR-Cas9 to express a model foreign antigen (e.g., ovalbumin) and defined, high-affinity native neoantigens derived from common driver mutations (e.g., mutant Kras G12D). Mice will be vaccinated against the foreign antigen, and T cell reactivity will be interrogated using peptide-MHC tetramers for the neoantigens and intracellular cytokine staining. Functionality will be tested via in vivo cytotoxicity assays against targets pulsed with neoantigen peptides and, definitively, by demonstrating that adoptive transfer of these neoantigen-specific T cells mediates regression of established, untreated tumors in naive hosts.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field from correlative clinical observations to a causal, mechanistic understanding of functional epitope spreading to native neoantigens. It will provide definitive proof-of-principle that a monovalent vaccine can initiate a broad, therapeutically relevant polyclonal T cell response, directly informing the design of next-generation vaccine strategies for cancers with low mutational burden like PDAC.
proposals.novelty: While epitope spreading has been observed, this study uniquely combines a genetically precise tumor model with rigorous, functional validation to prove that de novo primed CD8+ T cells against endogenous neoantigens are directly tumoricidal, not merely bystanders.

proposals.title: In Vivo Gene Editing of Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma (PDAC) Driver Genes Using Recombinant Adenoviral Vectors
proposals.motivation: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) remains a lethal malignancy with limited therapeutic options and a profound genetic understanding derived from the Vogelstein lab's work on genes like KRAS, TP53, CDKN2A, and SMAD4. While these genetic drivers are well-characterized, functional validation of their roles in established tumors and their potential as therapeutic targets in vivo is challenging. Current models, including genetically engineered mice, are time-consuming and lack the flexibility for rapid, combinatorial gene manipulation in autochthonous tumors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that recombinant, replication-deficient adenoviral vectors can be used to efficiently deliver gene-editing constructs (e.g., CRISPR-Cas9) directly into established PDAC cells in vivo, enabling the functional dissection of core driver genes and their downstream pathways within the native tumor microenvironment.
proposals.method: We will engineer high-titer, serotype 5 adenoviral vectors expressing CRISPR-Cas9 and specific sgRNAs targeting PDAC driver genes (e.g., mutant KRASG12D, TP53) under tissue-specific promoters. These vectors will be delivered via intraductal or intratumoral injection into established PDAC in genetically engineered KPC (LSL-KrasG12D/+; LSL-Trp53R172H/+; Pdx-1-Cre) mice. Tumor progression will be monitored by high-resolution ultrasound and histology. We will employ droplet digital PCR and next-generation sequencing of PCR-amplified tumor DNA from laser-capture microdissected slices to quantify editing efficiency and clonal dynamics, integrating this with RNA-seq from sorted tumor cells to assess pathway consequences.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust, rapid platform for in vivo functional genomics in autochthonous PDAC, moving beyond correlation to direct causation. It will provide definitive in vivo evidence for the therapeutic vulnerability of specific genetic alterations and their downstream effectors. The methodology can be extended to test combinatorial gene knockouts and deliver therapeutic transgenes, directly informing novel targeted and immune-based strategies.
proposals.novelty: While adenoviral vectors have been used for gene delivery, their application for precise, in vivo CRISPR-mediated editing of endogenous driver genes within the complex stroma of established, autochthonous PDAC is novel. This approach combines the Vogelstein lab's deep genetic knowledge of PDAC with direct in vivo perturbation, bypassing the need for extensive new mouse model generation for each genetic question.

proposals.title: Tumor-Specific Superantigen Gene Therapy for Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma Using Lentiviral Vectors
proposals.motivation: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) remains a lethal malignancy with limited therapeutic options and poor response to conventional immunotherapies. A critical need exists for novel, potent immunotherapeutic strategies that can overcome the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment. Bacterial superantigens are powerful T-cell activators but their systemic toxicity has precluded clinical use, necessitating a targeted delivery approach.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ex vivo transduction of PDAC cells with a lentiviral vector encoding a bacterial superantigen gene under a tumor-specific promoter, followed by reintroduction into the tumor microenvironment, will create a localized and potent anti-tumor immune response with reduced systemic toxicity.
proposals.method: We will construct a replication-deficient lentiviral vector carrying the Staphylococcus aureus enterotoxin B (SEB) gene under the control of the human telomerase reverse transcriptase (hTERT) promoter, a tumor-specific promoter active in PDAC. Patient-derived PDAC organoids or primary cell lines will be transduced ex vivo. Transduced cells will be re-implanted into immunocompetent, genetically engineered mouse models of PDAC (e.g., KPC mice). Tumor growth, immune infiltration (by flow cytometry for T-cell subsets and activation markers), and systemic cytokine levels will be monitored to assess efficacy and safety.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for a novel, tumor-localized superantigen immunotherapy platform for PDAC. It will provide critical pre-clinical data on the potency and safety profile of this approach, potentially identifying a new strategy to convert immunologically 'cold' PDAC tumors into 'hot' ones. The findings could be broadly applicable to other solid tumors.
proposals.novelty: While viral vectors have been used for cytokine or chimeric antigen receptor delivery, the application of a replication-deficient lentivirus to achieve tumor-restricted expression of a potent bacterial superantigen represents a novel fusion of gene therapy and superantigen biology. This approach specifically addresses the historical limitation of superantigen systemic toxicity by leveraging ex vivo cell engineering for localized delivery.

proposals.title: Hapten-Mediated Immunogenic Conversion of Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma: A Preclinical Therapeutic Strategy
proposals.motivation: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is notoriously immunologically 'cold' and resistant to checkpoint blockade, largely due to a lack of neoantigens and poor T-cell infiltration. Pre-2026 immunology established the hapten-carrier principle, where small molecules (haptens) become immunogenic when bound to large carrier proteins, provoking robust T-cell responses. This concept has not been systematically applied to create de novo tumor-specific antigens in established PDAC.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that intratumoral delivery of defined, non-toxic haptens will bind covalently to native PDAC cellular proteins, creating novel neoantigen-like epitopes that can be recognized as 'foreign' by the host immune system, thereby converting immunologically ignorant PDAC tumors into targets for cytotoxic T-cell attack.
proposals.method: We will utilize the well-characterized hapten 2,4-dinitrofluorobenzene (DNFB) in autochthonous KPC (LSL-KrasG12D/+; LSL-Trp53R172H/+; Pdx-1-Cre) mouse PDAC models. DNFB will be delivered via ultrasound-guided intratumoral injection. Tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes will be analyzed by flow cytometry for CD8+ T-cell activation and proliferation. We will employ our established in vivo cloning and exome sequencing pipelines to identify hapten-modified peptides presented on MHC-I by PDAC cells. Response will be correlated with mutational burden (e.g., Trp53 status) and compared to anti-PD-1 treatment.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish proof-of-concept for haptenization as a method to induce immunogenic conversion in solid tumors. It will identify the specific immune mechanisms and potential biomarkers (e.g., carrier protein identity) required for efficacy. The findings could directly inform the development of a novel, localized immunotherapeutic modality for PDAC.
proposals.novelty: While haptens are classic tools in immunology, their direct application as in situ tumor-modifying agents to create artificial T-cell epitopes in genetically engineered PDAC models is novel. This approach bypasses the need for identifying patient-specific neoantigens and repurposes a fundamental immunological concept for cancer therapy.

proposals.title: Functional Validation of PDAC Driver Genes Using Humanized Patient-Derived Xenograft Models
proposals.motivation: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) remains a lethal malignancy with limited therapeutic options, largely due to an incomplete understanding of the functional impact of its complex genetic landscape. While large-scale sequencing efforts have catalogued mutations in PDAC, the functional validation of candidate driver genes, particularly in a relevant human tumor microenvironment, is a critical bottleneck. Preclinical models that incorporate both human tumor cells and a human immune component are essential to accurately assess gene function and therapeutic vulnerability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CRISPR/Cas9-mediated knockout of specific, recurrently mutated genes identified in PDAC sequencing studies will differentially alter tumor growth and therapy response in humanized patient-derived xenograft (PDX) models, thereby validating their functional driver status.
proposals.method: We will establish PDX models from surgically resected PDAC tumors in immunodeficient NSG mice. A subset of mice will be humanized via engraftment of CD34+ hematopoietic stem cells to reconstitute a human immune system. Using lentiviral vectors, we will introduce CRISPR/Cas9 constructs targeting top candidate genes (e.g., RNF43, KDM6A) or non-coding regulatory elements into low-passage PDX-derived organoids prior to orthotopic re-implantation. Tumor growth will be monitored by high-resolution ultrasound, and endpoint analyses will include bulk RNA-seq, digital PCR for mutational burden, and multiplex immunohistochemistry to assess tumor-immune interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct functional evidence for the driver role of specific genetic alterations in PDAC within a physiologically relevant human tumor-immune context. It will establish a scalable platform for prioritizing therapeutic targets and for testing combination therapies, including immunotherapies, that are informed by the genetic makeup of the tumor.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond genetic correlation in cell lines or immunocompromised hosts by integrating CRISPR-based functional genomics with humanized PDX models, allowing for the simultaneous assessment of gene function on both tumor-intrinsic properties and the adaptive human immune response.

proposals.title: Systematic In Vivo CRISPR-Cas9 Screening for Antigen Presentation Suppressors in Immunogenic Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma
proposals.motivation: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is notoriously non-immunogenic and resistant to checkpoint blockade, yet the specific genetic drivers of this immune evasion remain incompletely mapped. While prior work has focused on identifying neoantigens, an inverse approach—directly suppressing antigen presentation in an immunogenic tumor to reconstitute escape—can pinpoint the most critical vulnerabilities in the immune recognition pathway. This is crucial for understanding therapeutic resistance and for developing strategies to re-sensitize tumors to immunotherapy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted genetic disruption of components within the MHC class I antigen presentation pathway, specifically in an immunogenic PDAC model, will be sufficient to drive immune escape and tumor progression in immunocompetent hosts.
proposals.method: We will employ an in vivo CRISPR-Cas9 screening approach. An immunogenic murine PDAC cell line, derived from a model with high mutational burden, will be transduced with a lentiviral sgRNA library targeting a curated set of ~200 genes involved in antigen processing and presentation (e.g., B2m, Tap1/2, components of the immunoproteasome). These engineered cells will be transplanted into immunocompetent syngeneic mice. Tumors will be harvested at endpoints, and sgRNA representation will be quantified by next-generation sequencing. Enriched sgRNAs will identify genes whose loss confers a selective growth advantage, indicating their role as suppressors of immunogenicity. Validation will use in vivo cloning of PCR-amplified sgRNAs from selected hits into fresh cells for individual mouse studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, functional hierarchy of antigen presentation genes required for immune control of PDAC. It will identify non-canonical regulators of immunogenicity beyond beta-2-microglobulin. The findings will provide a genetic blueprint of immune escape mechanisms, offering new potential targets for combination therapies aimed at restoring tumor immunogenicity.
proposals.novelty: This study inverts the classic search for immunogenic antigens by directly engineering immune escape, applying high-throughput in vivo CRISPR screening—a method recently advanced for cancer gene discovery—specifically to the problem of antigen presentation suppression in a solid tumor context.

proposals.title: Intratumoral Hyaluronidase Gene Therapy for Established Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma: A Preclinical Evaluation of Tumor Regression and Stromal Remodeling
proposals.motivation: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is characterized by a dense, hyaluronic acid (HA)-rich desmoplastic stroma that contributes to chemoresistance, immune exclusion, and high interstitial fluid pressure, promoting tumor progression. While enzymatic depletion of HA with PEGPH20 (pegvorhyaluronidase alfa) has shown promise in preclinical models and early-phase clinical trials, systemic delivery faces challenges with toxicity and inconsistent efficacy. A direct, localized genetic approach to degrade HA within the tumor microenvironment could provide sustained stromal modulation with reduced off-target effects.
proposals.hypothesis: Direct intratumoral transfer of a hyaluronidase (e.g., human PH20) gene into established PDAC tumors will lead to sustained enzymatic HA degradation, resulting in stromal remodeling, reduced tumor pressure, improved drug perfusion, and consequent tumor regression.
proposals.method: We will utilize a syngeneic orthotopic mouse model of PDAC (e.g., derived from KPC cells) to establish measurable tumors. The hyaluronidase PH20 cDNA will be cloned into a recombinant adeno-associated virus (rAAV) vector known for efficient transduction of pancreatic tissue. Tumors will be injected intratumorally with the rAAV-PH20 construct or control vectors. Tumor growth will be monitored by high-resolution ultrasound. Post-treatment, tumors will be analyzed for HA content (histology with HA-binding protein stain), stromal markers (α-SMA, collagen), intratumoral pressure, perfusion (via contrast-enhanced ultrasound), and immune cell infiltration (CD8+ T cells) using established immunohistochemistry and in vivo imaging protocols.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish proof-of-concept for intratumoral gene therapy as a novel strategy to durably modulate the PDAC stroma. It will provide critical in vivo data on the feasibility, efficacy, and biological consequences of localized HA degradation, informing future combinatorial approaches with chemotherapy or immunotherapy. The findings could redefine stromal targeting from a systemic, intermittent pharmacological intervention to a localized, sustained genetic one.
proposals.novelty: While systemic hyaluronidase has been tested, the direct genetic delivery of hyaluronidase into established tumors to create a sustained, localized enzymatic depot is a novel therapeutic strategy. This approach leverages Vogelstein's expertise in gene transfer and pancreatic cancer models to address a core resistance mechanism via a persistent, tumor-focused intervention.

proposals.title: Targeting Cancer-Associated Fibroblast Precursors to Initiate Antigen Spread and Anti-Tumor Immunity in Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma
proposals.motivation: Cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) are a dominant, immunosuppressive component of the pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) stroma, contributing to therapeutic resistance. While immunotherapies targeting tumor neoantigens have largely failed in PDAC, the stromal compartment represents a stable, non-evolving target. However, directly targeting mature CAFs has proven challenging due to their heterogeneity and plasticity. A precursor-targeting strategy could circumvent this by initiating immunity against a defined, pre-malignant stromal population.
proposals.hypothesis: Immunization against lineage-specific surface antigens on the dominant precursor cells that give rise to tumor-promoting CAFs will elicit a cytotoxic immune response that, through epitope spreading, broadens to target native tumor cell antigens, generating a comprehensive anti-tumor immune attack.
proposals.method: We will first lineage-trace CAF origins in autochthonous KPC mouse PDAC models using Col1a2-CreER;Rosa-LSL-tdTomato and Pdgfra-CreER systems, followed by single-cell RNA sequencing (10x Genomics) to identify precursor-specific surface markers. We will then vaccinate mice with mRNA-LNP constructs encoding these antigens and monitor T cell responses via intracellular cytokine staining and tetramer analysis. Antigen spread to tumor cells will be assessed by isolating tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes and testing reactivity against a library of previously identified PDAC neoantigens and shared tumor-associated antigens (e.g., Mesothelin, mutant Kras peptides) using IFN-γ ELISpot.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel stromal vaccination paradigm for solid tumors, moving beyond direct tumor cell targeting. It will provide definitive proof-of-concept that attacking the tumor's supportive niche can initiate a cascade of adaptive immunity against the cancer itself. The identified precursor-specific antigens could serve as new vaccine targets for PDAC and other stroma-rich carcinomas.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has aimed to deplete or re-educate CAFs, actively vaccinating against their precursors to *initiate* antigen spread is a novel immunologic strategy. This approach leverages the stability of the stromal compartment to overcome the antigenic instability and heterogeneity of the tumor cells, a concept not yet tested in vivo.

proposals.title: In Vivo Genetic Lineage Tracing Reveals the Cellular Origins and Fate of Cancer-Associated Fibroblasts in Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma
proposals.motivation: Cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) are a dominant and heterogeneous component of the PDAC tumor microenvironment, promoting tumor progression, metastasis, and therapeutic resistance. However, the precise cellular origins of CAF subtypes (e.g., inflammatory, myofibroblastic) and their dynamics during tumorigenesis remain poorly defined, limiting our ability to target them therapeutically. Resolving this lineage hierarchy is a critical unmet need in pancreatic cancer biology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, resident tissue-resident fibroblast populations, marked by specific genetic drivers, serve as the primary precursors for different CAF subtypes in PDAC, and that their lineage commitment is influenced by oncogenic signaling from epithelial tumor cells.
proposals.method: We will employ Cre-loxP-based, inducible genetic lineage-tracing systems, a technique refined in stem cell research, using mouse models of PDAC (e.g., KPC). We will cross PDAC-driver mice (Pdx1-Cre; LSL-KrasG12D; Trp53fl/+) with a panel of novel reporter mice where Cre recombinase is driven by promoters of candidate precursor genes (e.g., Gli1, Pdgfra, Acta2, Col1a2). Following tamoxifen-induced labeling at defined timepoints, we will use flow cytometry and single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) of dissociated tumors to track the labeled progeny and define their transcriptional profiles, quantifying their contribution to established CAF subtypes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a definitive lineage map for CAF subtypes in PDAC, identifying their cells of origin and differentiation trajectories. It will provide a functional, genetic framework to understand CAF heterogeneity, moving beyond correlative marker studies. The findings could reveal novel, lineage-specific targets for stromal-directed therapies.
proposals.novelty: While lineage tracing is established in developmental biology, its systematic application to deconvolute the tumor stroma in an autochthonous PDAC model is novel. This approach moves beyond static snapshot analyses to dynamically link CAF precursor identity with functional subtype fate during tumor evolution.

proposals.title: Defining the Instructive Signal: A Systematic Screen for Cancer-Secreted Factors Sufficient to Reprogram Pancreatic Fibroblasts into a CAF Phenotype
proposals.motivation: Cancer-Associated Fibroblasts (CAFs) are a dominant, pro-tumorigenic component of the pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) microenvironment, but their origin and the precise signals that drive their differentiation from normal fibroblasts remain poorly defined. Current studies primarily catalog correlations between tumor-secreted factors and fibroblast states, failing to distinguish instructive signals from permissive or secondary changes. This gap limits our ability to therapeutically intercept CAF education.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a specific combination of cancer-secreted factors, delivered in a defined temporal sequence and concentration, constitutes an instructive signal sufficient to reprogram a normal pancreatic stellate cell (PSC) into a stable, tumor-promoting CAF phenotype.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-throughput, systematic screening approach using conditioned media from primary human PDAC organoids and a library of recombinant proteins targeting known and candidate morphogens (e.g., TGF-β, IL-6, SHH, PDGF). Primary human PSCs will be exposed to these factors in varied combinations, sequences, and doses. Phenotypic reprogramming will be assessed via high-content imaging for established CAF markers (α-SMA, FAP) and functional assays (collagen contraction, invasion). Machine learning (e.g., random forest regression) will be applied to the multidimensional screening data to identify the minimal sufficient signal combination and its critical timing.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond association to establish a causal, molecular definition of the 'CAF education curriculum' in PDAC. It will identify the core set of tumor-derived instructive signals, providing a foundational map for future therapeutic targeting of CAF genesis. The resulting predictive model of fibroblast reprogramming will be a resource for the field.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying an inverse problem-solving framework from developmental biology to deconstruct tumor-stroma education, actively defining the sufficient instructive signal rather than passively observing correlative changes in fibroblast state.

proposals.title: Targeted Reprogramming of Pro-Tumorigenic Cancer-Associated Fibroblast Subpopulations in Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma Using Synthetic Genetic Circuits
proposals.motivation: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is characterized by a dense, heterogeneous stroma dominated by cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs). Pre-2026 single-cell RNA-seq studies have revealed distinct CAF subpopulations, with some (e.g., myofibroblastic CAFs) promoting tumor progression and therapy resistance. Current stromal-targeting strategies fail to discriminate between these pro-tumorigenic subsets and potentially tumor-restraining CAFs, limiting efficacy and causing toxicity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pro-tumorigenic CAF subpopulations in PDAC can be selectively identified by unique surface marker combinations and that deploying synthetic genetic sensor-actuator systems will enable their precise targeting and functional reprogramming to an anti-tumorigenic state.
proposals.method: We will first perform deep single-cell RNA sequencing and proteomic analysis on patient-derived PDAC organoids and mouse models to define targetable surface markers for specific CAF subsets. Using this data, we will design modular genetic circuits based on pre-2026 synthetic biology toolkits (e.g., synNotch receptors). These circuits will be delivered via engineered viral vectors to act as 'sensors' for subpopulation-specific marker combinations. Upon recognition, the circuits will trigger 'actuator' modules to express therapeutic payloads, such as secreted TNF-related apoptosis-inducing ligand (TRAIL) or interleukin-12, exclusively within the targeted CAF niche.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, precision stromal therapy paradigm for PDAC that directly addresses CAF heterogeneity. It will generate a validated genetic toolkit for context-aware stromal reprogramming and provide critical proof-of-concept that targeting specific CAF subsets, rather than the bulk stroma, can improve therapeutic outcomes in genetically engineered mouse models of PDAC.
proposals.novelty: While CAF heterogeneity is recognized, existing approaches broadly deplete or inhibit all CAFs. Our proposal is novel in its integration of patient-specific single-cell analytics with synthetic biology to create closed-loop, discriminatory genetic circuits that perform subpopulation-selective reprogramming within the tumor microenvironment.

proposals.title: Multiclonal Origin of Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma: Testing the Fusion-Cooperation Hypothesis in Genetically Engineered Mouse Models
proposals.motivation: The clonal evolution model, a cornerstone of cancer genetics, posits that tumors arise from a single transformed cell. However, this model cannot easily explain the profound intratumoral heterogeneity observed in cancers like pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC). Pre-2026 studies in inflammation and wound healing suggest that independent cellular clones can fuse or cooperate, raising the possibility that some malignancies might originate from multiple initiated cells, a hypothesis with major implications for understanding tumor initiation and therapeutic resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subset of PDACs originates not from a single clone, but from the fusion or cooperative interaction of two or more independent, initiated epithelial clones within the premalignant pancreatic lesion.
proposals.method: We will employ dual-recombinase genetically engineered mouse models (GEMMs) of PDAC (e.g., combining KrasG12D activation with Trp53 loss) using spatially restricted, inducible Cre and Flp drivers. By differentially labeling adjacent pancreatic ductal cells with distinct fluorescent reporters (e.g., GFP vs. tdTomato) and/or unique somatic CRISPR-Cas9-induced barcodes, we will trace clonal lineages. Tumors will be analyzed via high-resolution confocal microscopy of tissue slices and single-cell RNA/DNA sequencing. Computational analysis will use phylogenetic algorithms (e.g., MEDICC) on single-cell DNA-seq data to reconstruct distinct clonal lineages and assess their co-evolution within individual tumors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct in vivo experimental test of the multiclonal origin hypothesis in a major human cancer. It will establish a novel mechanistic framework for understanding early tumorigenesis and could identify multiclonality as a new biomarker for aggressive disease. The findings may challenge fundamental paradigms in cancer genetics and inform strategies for early interception.
proposals.novelty: While clonal cooperation in established tumors is recognized, the idea that the founding event of a carcinoma involves multiple independent clones is heretical to the prevailing monoclonality dogma. This proposal leverages advanced GEMMs and lineage-tracing to directly interrogate this non-canonical origin story at tumor inception.

proposals.title: Systematic Investigation of X-Chromosome Inactivation Escape as a Novel Hallmark and Diagnostic Confounder in Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, DNA methylation patterns, particularly on the inactive X chromosome (Xi), are used as clonal markers in cancer lineage tracing and minimal residual disease assays, assuming Xi methylation is stably inherited. However, the profound epigenetic dysregulation in cancers like pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) could destabilize this silencing, leading to Xi reactivation. This would undermine the premise of these diagnostic tools and potentially contribute to tumorigenesis through dosage effects of X-linked genes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the epigenetic stress of PDAC tumorigenesis can lead to partial or complete reactivation of the inactive X chromosome in female-derived cells, and that this event is a non-random, clonally selected feature contributing to tumor progression.
proposals.method: We will establish patient-derived organoid (PDO) lines from female PDAC patients and matched normal pancreatic ductal cells. Using allele-specific RNA-seq and chromatin conformation capture (Hi-C), we will assess expression and 3D architecture of the Xi. We will apply a custom analysis pipeline integrating bisulfite sequencing (BS-seq) data with RNA-seq to quantify allele-specific methylation and expression, building on our group's expertise in somatic mutation analysis. Functional validation will involve CRISPRa-mediated reactivation of candidate X-linked tumor suppressor genes (e.g., ATRX) in normal organoids to assess transformation potential.
proposals.contribution: This work will determine if Xi reactivation is a novel hallmark of PDAC, with immediate implications for the validity of methylation-based clonal tracking assays. It will identify specific reactivated X-linked genes that may act as drivers or vulnerabilities, opening new therapeutic avenues. The findings will redefine the stability of epigenetic inheritance in cancer.
proposals.novelty: While loss of imprinting is known in cancer, systematic investigation of Xi erosion in solid tumors, particularly PDAC, is lacking. The idea that a fundamental epigenetic silencing mechanism assumed stable for diagnostics could be actively subverted by the tumor is a provocative and under-explored concept.

proposals.title: A Pan-Cancer Panel of Highly Polymorphic Autosomal Genes for Universal Clonality Analysis in Human Tissues
proposals.motivation: Clonality analysis is crucial for distinguishing reactive from neoplastic processes in cancer diagnostics and for studying tumor evolution. Current methods, such as assays for X-chromosome inactivation or mitochondrial DNA mutations, are limited by gender specificity or low polymorphism rates, restricting their universal applicability. A robust, gender-neutral panel of highly polymorphic autosomal markers would enable clonality assessment in all patients, particularly for solid tumors like PDAC and colorectal cancer where early clonal events are diagnostically challenging.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a curated set of autosomal genes, selected for high heterozygosity and low linkage disequilibrium, can serve as universal, sensitive markers for clonality analysis across diverse human cancers and patient populations.
proposals.method: We will mine pre-2026 public databases (e.g., 1000 Genomes, gnomAD) to identify candidate autosomal genes with high single-nucleotide variant (SNV) density and allele frequency distributions favoring heterozygosity. Candidate loci will be validated using PCR and Sanger sequencing on matched normal and tumor DNA from a cohort of PDAC and colorectal cancer patients, leveraging Vogelstein lab expertise. A bioinformatics pipeline will be developed to analyze sequencing chromatograms for allelic imbalance, using established algorithms like Pyrosequencing or fragment analysis for quantification, to confirm clonal detection sensitivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, open-source panel of autosomal clonality markers, providing a universal tool for diagnostic pathology and cancer research. It will enable clonality studies in any patient, irrespective of gender, improving the differential diagnosis of challenging lesions and facilitating studies of tumor heterogeneity and clonal evolution in common carcinomas.
proposals.novelty: While clonality assays exist, a systematic, data-driven identification of optimal autosomal markers to create a universal, gender-neutral panel is novel. This approach moves beyond the limitations of X-inactivation or mitochondrial assays, offering a more robust and widely applicable genetic tool for clonal analysis.

proposals.title: Systematic Identification and Genomic Characterization of Polyclonal Precursor Lesions in Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma
proposals.motivation: The clonal evolution model of cancer, established by Vogelstein and others, posits that tumors arise from a single founder cell. However, the potential for polyclonal origins in human neoplasia, particularly in pre-malignant lesions like pancreatic intraepithelial neoplasia (PanIN), remains poorly explored. Investigating this could fundamentally reshape our understanding of early carcinogenesis and explain rapid tumor progression and heterogeneity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant subset of histologically defined, pre-malignant pancreatic lesions are polyclonal proliferations, originating from the cooperative expansion of multiple genetically distinct epithelial clones.
proposals.method: We will leverage our established in vivo cloning assay, where PCR-amplified genomic DNA from micro-dissected human PanIN lesions (from surgical or autopsy specimens) is cloned into a lambda phage vector and packaged for infection of E. coli, enabling the physical separation of individual mutant alleles. This will be combined with deep, multiplexed sequencing of key PDAC driver genes (KRAS, CDKN2A, TP53, SMAD4) from hundreds of single-clone isolates per lesion. A custom computational pipeline will analyze the co-occurrence and allelic frequencies of distinct, non-overlapping mutations to definitively assess clonality.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, high-resolution census of polyclonality in PDAC precursor lesions. It will establish whether polyclonal cooperation is a rare or common event in pancreatic carcinogenesis and identify the specific mutational combinations that facilitate cooperative clonal expansion.
proposals.novelty: While clonality assays exist, applying this sensitive in vivo cloning method to meticulously micro-dissected pre-malignant lesions, rather than established tumors, to directly test the monoclonality axiom at the earliest stages of human cancer is novel. It moves beyond inferring clonality from bulk sequencing data.

proposals.title: Clonal Analysis of Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma Precursor Lesions Using Polymerase Chain Reaction-Amplified DNA from Microdissected Tissue
proposals.motivation: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) has a dismal prognosis, largely due to late diagnosis. Understanding the clonal evolution of its precursor lesions, such as pancreatic intraepithelial neoplasia (PanIN), is critical for early detection and intervention. Current methods for clonality analysis, like Southern blotting, require large amounts of DNA, limiting studies of these small, architecturally complex lesions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the polymerase chain reaction (PCR), by enabling the amplification of target DNA sequences from minute tissue samples, can be used to determine the clonal origin and genetic heterogeneity of PDAC precursor lesions with a sensitivity and resolution not previously possible.
proposals.method: We will microdissect specific PanIN lesions and adjacent normal ductal cells from archival, formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded pancreatic tissue slices using a manual micromanipulator. DNA will be extracted, and PCR will be performed to amplify polymorphic loci, specifically variable number tandem repeats (VNTRs) and restriction fragment length polymorphisms (RFLPs) linked to known PDAC-associated genes (e.g., KRAS). The PCR products will be cloned in vivo into E. coli using M13 vectors, and multiple clones from each lesion will be sequenced to assess clonality based on the pattern of allelic loss or mutation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust, high-sensitivity method for clonal analysis of microscopic pre-neoplastic tissues. It will provide foundational data on the monoclonality versus polyclonality of PanINs, directly testing models of PDAC initiation. The technique will be a powerful tool for mapping the earliest genetic events in pancreatic carcinogenesis.
proposals.novelty: While PCR and clonality assays have been used in hematology and colorectal cancer, their combined application to microdissected, architecturally preserved pancreatic precursor lesions is novel. This approach directly addresses the technical bottleneck of sample scarcity that has hindered the molecular study of PDAC origins.

proposals.title: Defining the Clonal Architecture of Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma through Single-Cell DNA Sequencing of Primary Tumor Cells
proposals.motivation: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is characterized by extensive intratumoral heterogeneity, which complicates treatment and drives therapeutic resistance. Bulk sequencing of tumor DNA averages signals from multiple clones, obscuring the precise evolutionary history and the true driver mutations within founding and subclonal populations. Resolving this clonal architecture at single-cell resolution is critical for understanding early tumorigenesis and identifying targets for more effective interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that single-cell DNA sequencing of primary PDAC cells will reveal a precise, branched evolutionary tree, identifying a conserved set of truncal driver mutations in genes like KRAS, TP53, CDKN2A, and SMAD4, and will uncover subclonal mutations that contribute to phenotypic diversity and metastatic potential.
proposals.method: We will apply a single-cell whole-genome sequencing (scWGS) approach to freshly resected PDAC tumors. Individual nuclei will be isolated via fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) or microfluidics. Amplification will be performed using a robust, low-bias method such as Multiple Displacement Amplification (MDA). Sequencing libraries will be prepared and analyzed using established bioinformatics pipelines (e.g., HMMcopy, Ginkgo) for copy number variation (CNV) detection and single-nucleotide variant (SNV) calling. Phylogenetic trees will be reconstructed from the somatic profiles of hundreds of single cells using maximum parsimony and Bayesian methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution map of clonal lineages in primary PDAC, definitively separating truncal from subclonal events. It will establish a foundational resource for understanding tumor evolution and will identify potential subclonal therapeutic targets. The methodology will also serve as a blueprint for clonality analysis in other solid tumors.
proposals.novelty: While single-cell RNA sequencing is established, applying robust single-cell DNA sequencing to solid tumors like PDAC to directly assess clonality from genomic DNA—rather than inferring it from bulk data or transcriptomes—remains a significant technical and analytical challenge that this proposal directly addresses.

proposals.title: Automated, High-Throughput Digital PCR Platform for Population-Scale Screening of Clonal Hematopoiesis and Early Cancer-Associated Mutations
proposals.motivation: Clonal expansions driven by somatic mutations in genes like TP53, KRAS, and DNMT3A are hallmarks of cancer initiation and age-related clonal hematopoiesis (CHIP), detectable in blood years before diagnosis. Current assays for detecting these low-frequency variants, such as ddPCR or targeted sequencing, are labor-intensive and not scalable for screening large, asymptomatic cohorts to assess cancer risk. An automated, high-throughput platform is urgently needed to translate these molecular insights into population-level preventive strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a fully automated, microfluidic digital PCR (dPCR) platform, integrated with a robotic liquid handler and automated analysis pipeline, can robustly and cost-effectively screen thousands of blood DNA samples for a panel of cancer-associated clonal mutations, enabling large-scale epidemiological studies of cancer risk.
proposals.method: We will adapt and automate a multiplex droplet digital PCR (ddPCR) assay targeting recurrent hotspot mutations in TP53, KRAS, NRAS, and DNMT3A. A Hamilton STAR or equivalent robotic liquid handler will be programmed for automated sample plating, master mix assembly, and droplet generation. Post-PCR, droplets will be read on a QX200 droplet reader, and data analysis will be fully automated using a custom R/Python pipeline implementing a Gaussian mixture model for precise thresholding of positive/negative droplet clusters and calculation of mutant allele frequencies, with results directly uploaded to a laboratory information management system (LIMS).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, end-to-end automated workflow for population-scale somatic variant screening, dramatically increasing throughput and reducing operator variability. It will provide the technical foundation for prospective studies correlating clonal mutation burden with future cancer risk, moving liquid biopsy concepts from late-stage disease monitoring to early detection and prevention.
